_vars.raw_problem: Design an efficient sorting algorithm for a large dataset of customer records, considering the need for frequent searches by name, email, and phone number, while minimizing memory usage and ensuring the system can handle a high volume of concurrent updates.
_vars.student_framing: [{"section_type": "Problem Statement Refinement", "content": "The problem requires designing an efficient sorting algorithm for a large dataset of customer records. The records contain attributes such as name, email, and phone number, and the system needs to support frequent searches by these attributes. The algorithm should minimize memory usage and ensure the system can handle a high volume of concurrent updates."}, {"section_type": "Functional Requirements", "content": "The sorting algorithm should have the following properties: (1) efficient search by name, email, and phone number, (2) ability to handle a high volume of concurrent updates, (3) minimal memory usage, and (4) scalability to accommodate a large dataset of customer records."}, {"section_type": "Non-Functional Requirements", "content": "The algorithm should have a time complexity of O(n log n) or better for sorting, and a time complexity of O(log n) or better for search operations. The algorithm should also ensure data consistency and integrity in the presence of concurrent updates."}, {"section_type": "Dataset Characteristics", "content": "The dataset consists of customer records with the following attributes: name, email, phone number, and other relevant customer information. The dataset is expected to be large, with millions of records, and will be constantly updated with new records and changes to existing records."}, {"section_type": "System Constraints", "content": "The system has limited memory resources, and the algorithm should be designed to minimize memory usage. The system should also be able to handle a high volume of concurrent updates, with multiple users accessing and updating the dataset simultaneously."}, {"section_type": "Evaluation Metrics", "content": "The algorithm will be evaluated based on the following metrics: (1) search time complexity, (2) update time complexity, (3) memory usage, and (4) scalability. The algorithm should be compared to existing sorting algorithms, such as quicksort and mergesort, and should demonstrate significant improvements in terms of efficiency and scalability."}, {"section_type": "Potential Algorithmic Approaches", "content": "Potential algorithmic approaches for solving this problem include: (1) using a combination of sorting algorithms, such as quicksort and mergesort, (2) utilizing data structures such as balanced binary search trees or hash tables, and (3) implementing a distributed sorting algorithm to take advantage of multiple processing units."}]

criteria.name: Problem Definition
criteria.description: The student accurately defines the problem, identifying key elements such as input, output, and constraints.
criteria.excellent_indicator: Clearly states the problem, including all necessary parameters and constraints.
criteria.poor_indicator: Fails to identify key elements of the problem or misinterprets the problem statement.

criteria.name: Relevance to Sorting & Searching
criteria.description: The student demonstrates an understanding of how the problem relates to sorting and searching algorithms.
criteria.excellent_indicator: Effectively explains how sorting and searching concepts apply to the problem.
criteria.poor_indicator: Fails to recognize or explain the connection between the problem and sorting and searching algorithms.

criteria.name: Well-Defined Task
criteria.description: The student translates the ill-defined problem into a well-defined task with clear objectives and requirements.
criteria.excellent_indicator: Produces a well-defined task that is solvable using sorting and searching algorithms.
criteria.poor_indicator: Fails to create a solvable task or defines a task that is not relevant to the problem.

typical_errors: Not recognizing the relevance of sorting and searching algorithms

best_framing.base: Design an efficient sorting algorithm for a large dataset of customer records, considering the need for frequent searches by name, email, and phone number, while minimizing memory usage and ensuring the system can handle a high volume of concurrent updates.

entries.title: Dataset Size
entries.description: The dataset contains approximately 1 million customer records, with each record consisting of name, email, phone number, and additional demographic information.

entries.title: Data Distribution
entries.description: The data is uniformly distributed across different names, emails, and phone numbers, with no significant skewness that could affect the sorting algorithm's performance.

entries.title: Memory Constraint
entries.description: The system has a maximum of 16 GB of RAM available for the sorting algorithm, and the goal is to minimize memory usage while maintaining efficiency.

entries.title: Concurrent Updates
entries.description: The system expects to handle up to 100 concurrent updates per second, with each update potentially affecting the sorting order.
entries.addresses_gap: definition of 'high volume' of concurrent updates

entries.title: Search Requirements
entries.description: Frequent searches by name, email, and phone number require an average response time of less than 1 second, with a maximum response time of 2 seconds for 99% of searches.

entries.title: Sorting Efficiency
entries.description: The sorting algorithm's efficiency will be measured by its ability to sort the dataset within 10 minutes, with a maximum of 5% increase in memory usage during the sorting process.

entries.title: Search Performance
entries.description: The search performance will be evaluated based on the average response time for searches by name, email, and phone number, with a target average response time of less than 500 milliseconds.

entries.title: In-Scope Requirements
entries.description: The sorting algorithm must be designed to handle the specified dataset size, distribution, and search requirements, while minimizing memory usage and handling concurrent updates.

entries.title: Out-of-Scope Requirements
entries.description: The design of the user interface, data visualization, and other non-algorithmic aspects are outside the scope of this problem.

entries.title: Customer Records
entries.description: Customer records contain sensitive information and must be handled in accordance with relevant data protection regulations, such as GDPR or CCPA.

best_framing.coverage_notes: This framing addresses all the criteria in the provided rubric, including Problem Definition, Relevance to Sorting & Searching, and Well-Defined Task. It also covers the known internal gaps in the problem, such as dataset size and distribution, specific memory constraints, and definition of 'high volume' of concurrent updates.

criticism: The student's framing lacks specific details about the dataset size and distribution, which are crucial for designing an efficient sorting algorithm. For instance, the 'Dataset Characteristics' section only mentions that the dataset is 'expected to be large, with millions of records,' without providing a clear estimate. Additionally, the 'System Constraints' section states that the system has 'limited memory resources,' but does not specify the exact amount of memory available. The student also fails to provide a clear definition of what constitutes a 'high volume' of concurrent updates, which is essential for determining the algorithm's scalability.
better_alternative: A stronger framing would involve providing more specific details about the dataset size, distribution, and memory constraints. For example, the student could assume a dataset size of approximately 1 million customer records, with each record consisting of name, email, phone number, and additional demographic information. The student could also specify the exact amount of memory available, such as 16 GB of RAM, and define the 'high volume' of concurrent updates as up to 100 updates per second. Furthermore, the student could clarify the search requirements, such as an average response time of less than 1 second, with a maximum response time of 2 seconds for 99% of searches.

per_criterion_scores.internal_note: The student accurately identifies the key elements of the problem, but lacks specific details about the dataset size and distribution.
The student demonstrates an understanding of how the problem relates to sorting and searching algorithms, but does not provide a clear explanation of how these concepts apply to the problem.
The student translates the ill-defined problem into a partially defined task, but fails to provide clear objectives and requirements.

strengths: The student accurately identifies the key elements of the problem, including the need for efficient sorting and searching.
The student demonstrates an understanding of the importance of minimizing memory usage and handling concurrent updates.
refinements_correctly_selected: The student correctly identifies the need for efficient search by name, email, and phone number.
refinements_missed: The student fails to provide a clear definition of the dataset size and distribution.
The student does not specify the exact amount of memory available.
The student lacks a clear definition of what constitutes a 'high volume' of concurrent updates.
unnecessary_selections: The student's selection of 'scalability' as a requirement is not clearly justified, as it is not explicitly mentioned in the problem statement.
